OFFICE OF RESEARCH INTEGRITY
200 UNIVERSITY OFFICE BUILDING
RIVERSIDE, CA 92521

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care.
citations.narrative:
During USDA inspection, it was found that on January 15, 2026, laboratory personnel [working under the Principal
Investigator (PI) of the hummingbird study (AUP #81)], failed to communicate the distressed condition of a male wild-
caught Anna’s Hummingbird directly to the Attending Veterinarian (AV). Instead, the PI was informed and by the time the
PI reached the bird in cage #11 at approx.5:00 pm, it was in respiratory distress and then died. In a phone conversation
with the AV, she said she was not informed until 5:21 pm via an email from the PI, only after the death of the Anna's
hummingbird.
Before its death, the hummingbird was on its third day of acclimation to captivity. The wild hummingbird was trapped on
the morning of January 13, 2026, and placed in cage #11. There was no record of daily observation for that first day of
captivity for all six hummingbirds that were trapped. The food consumption log was also left completely blank for January
13, 2026, and no food consumption amounts were documented for that day for all six hummingbirds. On the second day
of captivity, January 14, 2026, 19 mLs of food consumption was recorded for the hummingbird in cage #11. On the third
and last day of the hummingbird’s life, the amount of food consumed that was reported to the AV by the PI was only 5
mLs. This amount is inconsistent with what was recorded (25 mLs) on the food consumption log. The last time any
observation or feeding was documented for the hummingbird in cage #11 was at 10:50 a.m. on January 15, 2026. The
hummingbird died approx. 6 hours later.
The necropsy/pathology report stated under “General Comments: This hummingbird was captured on 1/13/2026. It was
doing fine (i.e., ate normal amount of food, etc.), on 1/14/2026. On 1/15/2026, it ate only 5 mL of food, and around 5:00
pm, it was found to be in respiratory distress and died." "The cause of death could not be determined based on
histopathologic examination alone.” Again, this is not consistent with the 25 mLs recorded by laboratory personnel for the
hummingbird on January 15, 2026.
An effective method of direct and frequent communication with the AV must be established for timely diagnosis and
adequate veterinary intervention. Daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being, and a mechanism
of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health,
behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.
Correct by March 19, 2026, and forward.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.33(b)(3)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care. 
During USDA inspection, it was found that on January 15, 2026, laboratory personnel [working under the Principal 
Investigator (PI) of the hummingbird study (AUP #81)], failed to communicate the distressed condition of a male wild-
caught Anna’s Hummingbird directly to the Attending Veterinarian (AV). Instead, the PI was informed and by the time the 
PI reached the bird in cage #11 at approx.5:00 pm, it was in respiratory distress and then died. In a phone conversation 
with the AV, she said she was not informed until 5:21 pm via an email from the PI, only after the death of the Anna's 
hummingbird.  
 
Before its death, the hummingbird was on its third day of acclimation to captivity. The wild hummingbird was trapped on 
the morning of January 13, 2026, and placed in cage #11. There was no record of daily observation for that first day of 
captivity for all six hummingbirds that were trapped. The food consumption log was also left completely blank for January 
13, 2026, and no food consumption amounts were documented for that day for all six hummingbirds. On the second day 
of captivity, January 14, 2026, 19 mLs of food consumption was recorded for the hummingbird in cage #11. On the third 
and last day of the hummingbird’s life, the amount of food consumed that was reported to the AV by the PI was only 5 
mLs. This amount is inconsistent with what was recorded (25 mLs) on the food consumption log. The last time any 
observation or feeding was documented for the hummingbird in cage #11 was at 10:50 a.m. on January 15, 2026. The 
hummingbird died approx. 6 hours later. 
 
The necropsy/pathology report stated under “General Comments: This hummingbird was captured on 1/13/2026. It was 
doing fine (i.e., ate normal amount of food, etc.), on 1/14/2026. On 1/15/2026, it ate only 5 mL of food, and around 5:00 
pm, it was found to be in respiratory distress and died." "The cause of death could not be determined based on 
histopathologic examination alone.” Again, this is not consistent with the 25 mLs recorded by laboratory personnel for the 
hummingbird on January 15, 2026. 
 
An effective method of direct and frequent communication with the AV must be established for timely diagnosis and 
adequate veterinary intervention. Daily observation of all animals to assess their health and well-being, and a mechanism 
of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, 
behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.

Correct by March 19, 2026, and forward. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
